Integration, the VLSI Journal  
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/vlsi  
Enhancing cell delay accuracy in post-placed netlists using ensemble  
tree-based algorithms  
∗
Yassine Attaoui a, , Mohamed Chentouf a, Zine El Abidine Alaoui Ismaili b, Aimad El Mourabit c  
a Siemens EDA/CSD Calypto - Synthesis Solutions, Rabat, Morocco  
b Information, Communication and Embedded Systems (ICES) Team, Mohammed V University, Rabat, Morocco  
c System & Data Engineering Team, ENSA of Tangier, Abdelmalek Essaâdi University, Tangier, Morocco  
A R T I C L E I N F O  
A B S T R A C T  
Keywords:  
Nowadays, the ASIC design is increasing in complexity, and PPA targets are pushed to the limit. The lack of  
physical information at the early design stages hinders precise timing predictions and may lead to design  
re-spins. In previous work, we successfully improved timing prediction at the post-placement stage using  
the Random Forest model, achieving 91.25% cell delay accuracy. Building upon this, we further investigate  
the potential of Ensemble Tree-based algorithms, specifically focusing on ‘‘Extremely Randomized Trees’’ and  
‘‘Gradient Boosting’’, to close the gap in cell delay accuracy. In this paper, we enrich the training dataset with  
new 16 nm industrial designs. The results demonstrate a substantial improvement, with an average cell delay  
accuracy of 92.01% and 84.26% on unseen data. The average Root-Mean-Square-Error is significantly reduced  
from 12.11 to 3.23 and 7.76 on unseen data.  
Delay estimation  
Machine learning  
Tree-based algorithms  
Post-placement delay estimation  
Cell delay  
1. Introduction  
congestion. This, in turn, causes the EDA tools to miscalculate the  
physically related metrics such as wire length, capacitive loads, slew,  
Recently, there has been a growing focus on achieving high timing  
accuracy during early design stages. The behavior of post-placement  
gate-level timing becomes particularly crucial as it directly affects  
the subsequent place-and-route stages. Timing inaccuracies during the  
placement stage can have significant implications on static timing  
analysis (STA), potentially leading to false reports of timing violations  
and subsequent functional failures. These inaccuracies can result in  
sub-optimal clock network designs, excessive power consumption and  
clock skew. As per the routing process, the delay inaccuracy may lead  
to lengthened or shortened wires, thus impacting design power and  
causing design re-spin as well as increasing design costs.  
and RC parasitics. While the imprecision in cell and interconnect delays  
diminishes as we progress through the physical design process and the  
layout information is no longer estimated, enhancing timing predictions  
at an early stage remains essential.  
The primary source of inaccuracy in timing estimations comes  
mainly from the lack of routing information. In Fig. 1, we showcase an  
example of post-placed cell delays for a 16 nm commercial design. The  
blue dots represent Oasys-RTL delay estimations difference from the  
accurate delay obtained from Aprisa P&R. We realize clearly the worst  
estimations deviate significantly exceeding 150 ns. Such misestimations  
can result in an over-designed layout, leading to unnecessary power and  
area consumption. As accurate timing information is available till after  
detailed routing, the estimations made during previous stages affect the  
design quality and functionality.  
Achieving timing closure in the early stages is crucial and var-  
ious pre-placement timing optimization techniques are employed to  
enhance timing and alleviate timing critical paths. These techniques  
encompass logic reconstruction, timing-driven synthesis, technology  
mapping, gate-sizing, buffering, and clock-gating, among others, all  
aim to reduce delays on critical paths and ensure compliance with  
timing constraints [1–3]. Nevertheless, these techniques are effective in  
improving timing at the current design stage, however, they may not  
fully considering the impact on subsequent stages and overall design  
performance.  
Recent studies tend to apply machine learning algorithms on down-  
stream stages by training models using a physically aware netlist and  
then using the trained model to attenuate the inaccuracy of estimations  
in upstream design stages. The focus of this paper is to enhance the  
timing accuracy during the post-placement stage by leveraging machine  
learning Ensemble Tree-based algorithms. We attempt to improve cell  
delay accuracy of Oasys-RTL Synthesis tool (Siemens EDA) [4] for post-  
placed gate netlist based on equivalent netlist generated using physical  
Additionally, the lack of physical information at early stages can  
result in over-pessimism which can cause sub-optimal routing and

Integration 97 (2024) 102193  
times, and cell/wire delay, was tested on designs ranging from 30K to  
150K instances, demonstrating potential in mitigating misestimations.  
In [9], the study explores a predictive timing model, specifically  
correlating GBA and PBA results. It uses CART to predict PBA arrival  
times and slacks, utilizing a dataset of 1.7 million samples with 13  
features from GBA. The dataset comprises in-house artificial designs  
and five medium-sized real designs. The research highlighted that  
dropping one feature led to a 2% drop in accuracy, while dropping  
two features resulted in a 4% decrease, indicating that incorporating  
more features could enhance accuracy. Our study aims to find a balance  
between maximizing the number of features and minimizing runtime,  
thus achieving a lower runtime with a higher number of features.  
[10] introduces a learning-based approach for detecting memory  
timing failures from the pre-placement stage, utilizing models like  
Lasso, SVM, Gradient Boosting, ANN, and HSM to predict post-layout  
slack. The study analyzed 7 small-sized 28 nm netlists, each not ex-  
ceeding 200K instances, and training models on 20 features derived  
from netlists, floorplans, and constraints. [11] predicts crosstalk-prone  
nets, coupling capacitance, crosstalk noise, and incremental delay using  
algorithms such as LR, ANN, RF, and XGBoost to improve design  
routability and timing at the routing stage, focusing on 12 benchmark  
designs at the 7 nm, with instances ranging from 3.5K to 74K. The  
models, trained on 20 net features including topology, wire delay, slew,  
and electrical/logical aspects like capacitance and fan metrics, found  
XGBoost to be the most accurate, achieving around 87% accuracy in  
incremental delay prediction.  
Fig. 1. Analyzing Oasys delay inaccuracy in comparison to Aprisa cell delays.  
Fig. 2. ML-based Oasys-RTL timing improvement.  
In [12], the author developed a model to improve switching power  
estimation using back-annotated RC parasitics from SPEF files, aiming  
for a ‘‘Spefless flow’’ that predicts cell switching power without relying  
on SPEF files. The model was trained with 80% of data from a single  
test, which might limit its generalization across different designs. Uti-  
lizing Linear Regression, Random Forest, and Decision Tree algorithms,  
the model, despite limited training data, showed high accuracy and  
significantly minimized errors in switching power predictions.  
aware P&R tool Aprisa PnR (Siemens EDA) [5]. Our approach consists  
of training various Supervised regression models on a set of 16 nm  
industrial designs to close the error gap on cell delays. To ensure fair  
comparison, we strive to keep the similarly netlists, although every tool  
employs its own placement and optimization techniques.  
To commence, we create the Oasys Virtual-Netlist, which is sub-  
sequently input to Aprisa for additional enhancements. Concurrently,  
Oasys executes its own optimizations to produce the Oasys Final Netlist.  
Eventually, we extract cell delays from both the final Oasys netlist and  
the Aprisa post-placed netlist, as depicted in Fig. 2.  
In [13], a pre-routing timing prediction employing Graph Neural  
Networks (GNN) was developed to enhance net delay and pin arrival  
times without the need for external router and timer engines. The  
study utilized 21 benchmarks from the 130 nm library, dividing them  
into 14 for training and 7 for testing, with a total training dataset of  
920K samples, achieving an average accuracy of 94%. Similarly, [14]  
developed an ANN and SVM-based models to predict slew, incremental  
delay, and path delay related to Signal Integrity (SI), tackling timing  
discrepancies between SI-mode and non-SI-mode. The models were  
trained on a dataset of 6 designs, with instance sizes ranging from  
6.5K to 350K. [15] emphasizes the impact of timing optimization  
on the pre-routing timing estimations. The model introduced com-  
bines Graph Neural Network (GNN) and Convolutional Neural Network  
(CNN) to integrate layout and netlist information. The GNN model  
extracts and aggregates netlist information for each endpoint, whereas  
the CNN discerns specific layout characteristics for each endpoint. This  
strategy allows the net-cell delays prediction following post-timing  
optimization and netlist restructuring, including after modifications  
and re-labeling. The prediction accuracy is assessed by comparing it the  
sign-off delays, achieving an average R2 score of 87%. [16] introduced  
a post-placement net delay predictor that generates a look-ahead RC  
network. This Random Forest-based model is trained on cell and net-  
related features to accurately predict parasitic resistance, capacitance,  
net delays, and critical path slack. The results show a strong correlation  
with pre-route sign-off STA results. [17] addressed gate delay discrep-  
ancies between single-input switching (SIS) and multi-input switching  
(MIS). Employing Polynomial Regression, SVR, and ANN, the study  
trained models on gate attributes such as output load and slew. The  
models were then integrated into the timing library for timing correc-  
tions. The ANN model has significantly reduced delay misestimations  
from 120% in SIS to 3%.  
The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Section 1 provides an  
overview of the current state-of-the-art techniques for improving timing  
accuracy in VLSI design using machine learning, along with a review  
of related works in this area. In Section 2, we describe the dataset and  
flow used and give a brief background on ML algorithms employed,  
and outline the methodology we followed for data processing, model  
training, testing, and evaluation. In Section 3, we present the results of  
our experiments. Finally, in Section 4, we draw conclusions based on  
our findings and discuss potential future research.  
2. Related work  
Machine learning has increasingly been applied to timing predic-  
tion, as evidenced by recent research. This section reviews various  
learning -based strategies for timing improvement, differentiating our  
method from existing state-of-the-art approaches.  
[6] developed a learning models using ANN, SVR, and Random  
Forest to rectify timing discrepancies in 28 nm and 45 nm designs, the  
study aims to reduce timing divergences in flip-flop setup time, cell arc  
delay, wire delay, stage delay, and path slack at timing endpoints. [7]  
introduced models employing Lasso, Neural Networks, and Random  
Forest for pre-routing timing prediction, significantly reducing timing  
pessimism in 45 nm designs’ net delays. The models were validated  
on 20 circuit implementations, with up to 45K nets. [8] focused on  
reducing net delay divergence and slew uncertainty using a learning-  
based model to correlate with signoff STA tool estimations. The model,  
trained on features like slew transition time, actual/required arrival  
2
     
Y. Attaoui et al.  
While prior research on early-stage timing improvement predom-  
Integration 97 (2024) 102193  
inantly focused on small to medium-sized designs from higher tech-  
nology nodes (28 nm and 45 nm), exploration of larger designs has  
been limited. These studies often utilized methods such as Random  
Forests, Gradient-based models, and Neural Networks, achieving high  
prediction accuracy but generally depending on a restricted set of  
features, approximately 20 parameters, which could potentially affects  
the accuracy. In contrast, our study encompasses 7 designs from the  
16 nm technology node, with most designs surpassing 430K instances,  
demonstrating a significant increase in scale and complexity. Our train-  
ing set, exceeding 2.3 million samples, marks a considerable extension  
beyond previous studies, leading to an enhanced cell delay accuracy of  
around 92%.  
Building upon our previous work [18], which primarily utilized  
the Random Forest model to enhance timing accuracy in both pre-  
placement and post-placement stages, our current study adopts ad-  
vanced tree-based algorithms. These include Extremely Randomized  
Trees (Extra-Trees), Gradient Boosting (GB), Light Gradient Boost-  
ing (LightGBM), eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), and Adaptive  
Boosting (AdaBoost). Moving forward, we plan to focus on deep learn-  
ing neural networks and introducing new design methodologies, which  
will be elaborated in an upcoming publication. This work contributes  
several key advancements:  
Fig. 3. Oasys Aprisa placement flow.  
• This work is the first of its kind to predict post-placement cell  
delays for large 16 nm industrial designs with diverse implemen-  
tations.  
stored in a CSV file. Furthermore, we have discarded net delays during  
this study and it will be addressed in a separate paper. For the purposes  
of our analysis, We directed both tools to execute using the parameters  
‘‘0-RC’’ and ‘‘0-LOAD’’ sequentially.This configuration assumes perfect  
interconnects with no delays attributed to the nets, enabling us to  
concentrate exclusively on the cell delays and their associated timing  
arcs.  
• Utilizing a comprehensive set of 45 cell and net attributes, the  
models are trained to accurately capture cell behavior and char-  
acteristics.  
• The predictions are based on timing arcs, cell unateness and arc  
sense attributes.  
• The model parameters are carefully tuned to optimize their per-  
formance, leading to the selection of the best hyperparameters.  
• The proposed models demonstrate strong generalization capa-  
bilities, effectively handling unseen data and maintaining their  
accuracy.  
3.2. Initial timing discrepancies  
The presence of physically aware parameters such as wire length,  
resistance, and capacitance, makes Aprisa trustworthy. As we aim to im-  
prove the convergence of delays at the post-placement without running  
the full physical design steps, we minimized the netlists differences.  
With the help of multiple tree-based algorithms, we conducted the  
training using a large dataset built from six 16 nm designs of different  
sizes, as detailed in Table 1. The total number of standard cells in each  
design reflects its size. Initial delay discrepancies between Oasys and  
Aprisa are visualized in scattered plots as in Fig. 4. The plots reflect  
the Oasys-RTL cell rise delay and Aprisa cell rise delay per design, as a  
matter of space we did not include the fall delays figures. The plotting  
includes a heatmap which refers to the cell’s output capacitance. In the  
legend, we included the initial Oasys and Aprisa Mean and Standard  
deviation.  
3. Designs characterization and ML algorithms  
Our approach consists of correcting Oasys-RTL cell delay estimation  
by leveraging multiple Tree-based machine-learning algorithms. Our  
objective is to compare different Ensemble Tree-based models using a  
carefully cleaned dataset. Before training and testing the models, we  
preprocessed the dataset by removing outliers and fine-tuned the hyper-  
parameters for each model to identify the parameters that contribute  
to the best estimation performance. In this section, we present the  
workflow we followed, including a detailed description of the dataset  
composition and the extracted features.  
3.1. Methodology  
3.3. Data pre-processing  
Our methodology consisted of executing two distinct workflows:  
one employing the Oasys-RTL Synthesis tool, and the other utilizing  
Aprisa P&R. Fig. 3 illustrates the process followed in each of these  
flows. In the Oasys-RTL flow [4], we started by feeding the design’s  
RTL database, constraints, and predefined floorplan. The tool then  
performed synthesis, technology mapping, and timing-area optimiza-  
tions. Afterward, the Synthesis tool generates a virtual unplaced gate  
netlist. In Aprisa P&R flow [5], we used the previously generated  
virtual unplaced netlist as input. Aprisa P&R continue with additional  
placement and attractively optimizing the design until closing timing.  
To minimize netlist modification, we instructed Aprisa to perform  
placement with low effort. Finally, we extracted cell and net delays,  
and various cell and net attributes, from both tool. All the collected  
features, including cell delays per timing arcs and other metrics, were  
In this paragraph, we describe the data pre-processing steps per-  
formed before training models. Our data collection process involved  
generating a series of CSV files from both tools. The CSV files en-  
compass a wide array of crucial information, including cell and net  
properties, the cell-net mapping connections, cell library attributes,  
and various physical metrics like cell input/output transitions, output  
capacitances, and delays. By employing an inner join on the cell’s full  
name, from pin and to pin columns, we effectively consolidated all the  
individual CSV files into a unified master CSV. These specific columns  
were designated as indexes (IDs), serving to uniquely identify each  
timing arc. As part of the data preparation process, certain columns  
that exhibited limited informative value for our predictive purposes  
were excluded from the master CSV.We leveraged the Scikit-learn,

The tool predicts delays for both positive and negative signal transi-  
tions, termed as cell delay rise and cell delay fall respectively. As a result,  
our initial focus was on training ML models to predict the delay rise  
attribute, which corresponds to positive unate arcs. Subsequently, we

Machine Learning provides the capacity to unveil correlations  
among patterns and detect trends within intricate data structures. Our  
objective is to utilize ML techniques to unveil the relationships between  
the extracted cell delay and various other cell attributes. The predicted  
cell delay is a continuous numerical float value, which requires the  
use of supervised regression models. In our previous work [18], we  
applied various state-of-the-art regression models, including Linear  
Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, Support Vector Regression,  
and K-Nearest Neighbors. After evaluating their performance, we found  
that Random Forest had the highest predictive score.  
3.5. Model features  
Compared to our previous study, we have reduced the number of  
relevant cell-net features from 101 to 45, as most of the cell and net  
extracted attributes were not contributing during model prediction. The  
current study is conducted on a reduced number of features, this will  
help by gaining much training and inference runtime.  
The collected datasets comprise a variety of categorical input vari-  
ables, we have performed feature encoding by converting categorical  
columns into numerical integers and boolean types to integers.  
As an example, the Arc Sense and Arc Type columns have a limited  
number of distinct values. We opted to treat these columns as enumer-  
ate type. Which involved creating new integer-type columns for each  
distinct value. The number of such additional columns corresponds  
to the count of distinct states. As a result, the dataframes result in  
a composition of 34 integer columns, 11 floating-point columns, and  
successful conversion of all categorical types into integers.  
Our current work introduces several advancements. Firstly, we  
expanded our exploration by applying ensemble methods Tree-based al-  
gorithms, extending the comparison beyond our previous best-  
performing model, the Random Forest. Secondly, we enhanced our  
analysis by incorporating two additional larger designs. Furthermore,  
in this study, we significantly refined the extracted features to a subset  
of 45 cell and net features. Finally, we tested the model’s generalization  
capability using larger design (TC7). Table 1 delineates the size of  
each design in terms of the number of standard cells, highlighting the  
increased complexity and scale of the designs considered in this study.  
The selected tree-based models include ‘Extremely Randomized  
Forests (Extra-Trees)’, ‘Gradient Boosting (GB)’, ‘Light Gradient Boost-  
ing (LightGBM)’, ‘eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)’, and ‘Adaptive  
Boosting (AdaBoost)’ [21–23]. These algorithms are decision tree based  
structures, it partitions the inputs into regions. The internal nodes  
corresponds to features and each branch is called a decision. Each  
decision assigns a predicted value to each leaf fof the tree [24–26].  
The Gradient Boosting techniques are built upon weak decision tree  
learners, it minimizes the errors of previous decision gradually until  
refining the overall model through the combination of weak learners.  
However, the Extra Trees (Extremely Randomized Trees) are ensemble  
methods that randomly select feature subsets for node and introduces  
additional randomness to mitigate variance and potential overfitting  
within the model [27,28].  
3.6. Dataset refinement  
As a crucial step in data pre-processing, the removal of outliers  
is imperative to ensure the robustness and reliability of the dataset.  
Outliers refer to data points that deviate significantly from the majority  
of the dataset, often caused by miscalculations or other anomalies. In  
our approach, we began by plotting scatter plots for several floating-  
type attributes, including cell output capacitance, input slew fall, output  
slew fall, input slew rise, output slew rise, delay rise, and delay fall. These  
scatter plots allow us to visually identify any data points that appear  
as outliers.  
Subsequently, we employed three different methods for outlier re-  
moval: ‘‘Z-score’’, ‘‘DBSCAN’’ and ‘‘Interquartile Range (IQR)’’. The  
focus is to get the highest accuracy while minimizing the number of  
removed outliers from the dataset. We have fine-tuned each method to  
identify the threshold beyond which the data is considered an outlier.  
We implemented Z-score at 3-4-5 standard deviation from the mean.  
Similarly, for IQR, we explored different quartile ranges, such as 75-  
25, 85-15, and 95-5, to identify the range at which outliers became  
prominent. We utilized the Interquartile Range (IQR) approach with  
a 4-sigma cutoff. This allowed us to establish up and low boundaries  
based on the mean value. Cells that fell beyond these bounds were con-  
sidered outliers and subsequently discarded. Through iterative analysis,  
we discovered that the optimal approach involves initially applying the  
IQR method with a range of 85-15 to identify and discard the initial  
set of outliers that exceed the 4-sigma threshold. Subsequently, we  
proceeded to apply the Z-score method at the 4th standard deviation.  
Table 4 provides a summary of the percentage of outliers for each  
design.  
To evaluate the quality of predictions, we utilize Root-Mean-  
Squared-Error and R2-score metrics. The RMSE function serves to assess  
the performance of each model by calculating the average magnitude  
of the errors produced, thereby providing a measure of the overall  
error rate. It is calculated by taking the square root of the mean of  
the squared differences between the predicted values and the actual  
values. A lower RMSE indicates better accuracy of the model. (Eq. (1)).  
Additionally, we employ the R2-score to evaluate the overall accuracy  
of the predictions. The R2 score, also known as the coefficient of  
determination, measures the variance in the target variable. A higher  
R2 score represents a more accurate alignment of the model predictions  
with the target values (Eq. (2)). In the next section, we compare each  
model’s R2 and RMSE metrics and drew a conclusion [29].  
The timing arcs that were considered outliers were removed from  
the TC1 to TC6 dataframes. While TC7 is considered intact raw data.  
In Fig. 5, we present a scatter plot after the removal of outliers, where  
the ꢎ-axis represents Oasys delays and the ꢏ -axis represents the Aprisa  
delays. We included a heatmap visualization to highlight the output  
capacitance. The scattered dots in the plot illustrate the discrepancy  
between Oasys-RTL’s cell delay compared to Aprisa delays.

4.1. Data analysis  
Percentage of outliers per dataframe.

Outliers

Remaining  
The cell delays mean and standard deviation (stdev) are calculated  
and presented in Table 5. After refining the data and removing outliers,  
the initial mean and stdev are 16.44 and 7.74, respectively. These  
values deviate considerably from Aprisa mean and stdev, which stand  
at 22.09 and 11.27, respectively. To quantify the error in Oasys cell  
delays, we computed the root-mean-squared-error (RMSE), with the  
average initial Oasys RMSE being 12.119, as shown in Table 5. This  
error metric provides insight into the overall accuracy of the Oasys  
estimations.

Fig. 4 illustrates the initial delay comparison, including outliers,  
where certain outlier values significantly deviated from the expected  
data range, consequently impacting delay prediction. Subsequently,  
Fig. 6 displays refined delays after outlier removal, with noticeable al-  
terations in the axis scales to accommodate these changes. Additionally,  
we remark a significant decrease in Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)  
following the removal of outliers as detailed in Tables 3 and 6. This  
notable reduction in RMSE emphasizes the substantial impact of outlier  
removal on enhancing the predictive accuracy of the model.  
Next, we applied a 70-15-15 training-validation-testing split to each  
design from TC1 to TC6 using train_test_split function from Scikit-learn  
library. Each model was trained using the specific design dataset.  
The performance metrics are summarized in Table 6. The validation  
set was utilized to determine the optimal hyperparameters that yield  
the highest model accuracy. This process was carried out using the  
GridSearchCV function from the scikitlearn library. The purpose of this  
analysis was to examine how each model performs with different design  
architectures, referred as Trial 1 in Table 2. By training and evaluating  
the models on individual designs, we aimed to gain insights into their  
behavior and effectiveness in capturing the nuances of specific design  
characteristics.  
4. Experimental results  
In the previous section, we presented a scattered plot comparing the  
initial Oasys delays with Aprisa delays (Fig. 5). The plot was generated  
after cleaning each design dataset by removing outliers. In this section,  
we focus on the results obtained after training, validating, and testing  
each model. We analyzed these results to identify the model that  
achieved the highest accuracy. Furthermore, we trained new models  
using the combined dataset from all test cases and evaluated their  
performance on unseen data.  
Afterwards, the dataset was subjected to random shuffling and  
divided into a 75-25 training-validation split. The resulting combined  
dataset is denoted as ‘‘Bunch’’ and is referred to as such in Tables 7. To  
assess the generalization capability of the models, we evaluated their  
performance on new unseen data, specifically TC7. The models were  
exclusively tested on TC7 to gauge their ability to generalize beyond  
the training dataset. The R2 scores obtained on the testing set, using

the best estimators identified by GridSearchCV on the validation set,

per each model. It is noteworthy that the models’ performance varies  
across different designs, reflecting the influence of the internal design  
architecture on the predictions. Notably, all models show improved  
accuracy compared to our previous work [18].  
4.2. Prediction results  
We observe that the top three models with the highest accuracy are  
Extra-Trees Random Forest, Random Forest, and AdaBoost, with average  
accuracies of 92.01%, 91.47%, and 91.23%, respectively. On the other  
hand, XGBoost, LightGB, and GradientBoosting show lower accuracy with  
average values of 90.66%, 90.40%, and 89.00%, respectively. These  
ensemble learning techniques, specifically based on gradient boosting,  
are less suitable for our data.  
Table 6 provides a summary of the accuracy scores achieved by  
each model and the corresponding RMSE. These scores were obtained  
after a hyper-parameter tuning process using GridSearch, which re-  
quired significant computational runtime to identify the best estimator  
model parameters. Due to space limitations, we intentionally omitted  
discussing the intricate details of hyperparameter tuning and the vali-  
dation scores. Instead, the focus was directed towards presenting the  
achieved scores utilizing the testing data and using best parameters  
In Figs. 7 and 8, we present a visual comparison of the R2-score and  
RMSE for each model in the form of histograms. It is worth noting that

Fig. 9. Features selection for models trained using bunch dataset.  
the error is inversely proportional to the R2-score. Among the models,  
Extra-Trees Random Forest, Random Forest, and AdaBoost exhibit the  
lowest RMSE values of 3.23, 3.28 and 3.31. On the other hand, XGBoost,  
LightGB, and GradientBoosting show higher errors with average values  
of 2.28, 3.43 and 3.65.  
data, which is quite promising. Fig. 6 portrays across six subplots the  
outcomes of TC7 testing set per model. The plots reveal a more cohesive  
distribution of predicted values, exhibiting enhanced performance as  
they align closely with the golden value.  
Even though the eXtreme Gradient Boosting and Extra-Tree Random  
Forest models achieved high scores when trained using the Bunch  
dataset, we also considered their training and inference times. The  
Extra-Tree RF model took around 10 h to train and predict cell delays,  
as shown in Table 9, while the eXtreme GB model only required 30 min  
for the same task. On the other hand, the Random Forest model took  
longer, lasting around 15 h, therefore, considering the balance between  
model prediction accuracy and runtime, the eXtreme Gradient Boosting  
and Extra-Tree Random Forest models seem more practical choices for  
our application. All models were executed on the same machine with  
identical CPU and memory configurations.  
Similarly, the models were trained using the combined dataframe  
(’Bunch’), and tested using TC7 data. Among the models, the eXtreme  
Gradient Boosting model achieved the highest accuracy of 84.321%,  
followed closely by the Extra-Tree RF with an accuracy of 84.266%.  
It is important to note that the obtained scores for TC7 are lower than  
the average in Table 6. This is expected, as TC7 represents completely  
unseen data and might encompass outliers capable of influencing the  
model’s predictive outcomes. However, despite the new data, the mod-  
els still delivered a relatively high level of stability and predictive  
capability, with accuracy ranging between 83% and 84% for unseen  
9
     
Y. Attaoui et al.  
Integration 97 (2024) 102193  
Ultimately, we conducted feature selection on models trained with

ing strategy for post-placement power optimization of IoT devices, in: 2021

Bunch dataset to emphasize the significant attributes influencing delay  
prediction. In Fig. 9, we present the selected features per model basis.  
During training process, we initially considered a pool of 45 features.  
However, for the sake of clarity, the complete set of features is not  
displayed in Fig. 9. Instead, we exclusively highlights the subset of  
features that exhibited higher contributions in the delay prediction.

and impact on power optimization during synthesis flow, in: 2021 International

[4] Siemens EDA oasys-RTL™ user’s guide, 2021.

[6] S.-S. Han, A.B. Kahng, S. Nath, A.S. Vydyanathan, A deep learning methodology  
5. Conclusion  
to proliferate golden signoff timing, in: 2014 Design, Automation  
Europe Conference & Exhibition, DATE, IEEE, 2014, pp. 1–6.  
&
Test in  
[7] E.C. Barboza, N. Shukla, Y. Chen, J. Hu, Machine learning-based pre-routing  
timing prediction with reduced pessimism, in: 2019 56th ACM/IEEE Design  
Automation Conference, DAC, IEEE, 2019, pp. 1–6.  
In this paper, we focused on applying Ensemble methods and Tree-  
based techniques to enhance the accuracy of cell delays in a post-placed  
netlist. Our approach involved training and testing six ML models using  
16 nm industrial designs, leveraging the Aprisa backend information  
as golden data for comparison. Through our proposed models, we  
achieved higher accuracy and lower error rates, with the ‘‘Extra-Tree  
Random Forest’’ and ‘‘Random Forest’’ models exhibiting the high-  
est accuracy among all models. However, in terms of generalization  
capability on unseen data, the ‘‘Extra-Tree Random Forest’’ as well  
as the ‘‘Extreme Gradient Boosting’’ models demonstrated the highest  
prediction accuracy.  
[8] A.B. Kahng, S. Kang, H. Lee, S. Nath, J. Wadhwani, Learning-based approxima-  
tion of interconnect delay and slew in signoff timing tools, in: 2013 ACM/IEEE  
International Workshop on System Level Interconnect Prediction, SLIP, IEEE,  
2013, pp. 1–8.  
[9] A.B. Kahng, U. Mallappa, L. Saul, Using machine learning to predict path-  
based slack from graph-based timing analysis, in: 2018 IEEE 36th International  
Conference on Computer Design, ICCD, IEEE, 2018, pp. 603–612.  
[10] W.-T.J. Chan, K.Y. Chung, A.B. Kahng, N.D. MacDonald, S. Nath, Learning-based  
prediction of embedded memory timing failures during initial floorplan design,  
in: 2016 21st Asia and South Pacific Design Automation Conference, ASP-DAC,  
IEEE, 2016, pp. 178–185.  
Using Ensemble methods, combining Tree-based and Gradient-based  
algorithms, we successfully reduced the average RMSE from 12.119 to  
3.23, accompanied by an impressive average accuracy of 92.01%. Both  
the ‘‘Extra-Tree RF’’ and ‘‘Extreme GB’’ models show higher stability  
and predictive capability on new unseen testing data, achieving an  
average accuracy of 84.26% lowering the average RMSE to 7.768.  
With this model, precise delay estimations can be obtained without  
relying on physical information, utilizing only attributes within the  
Oasys synthesis tool and delivering accurate delays within a short  
inference time. As extension of this work, we aim to improve the  
training set by incorporating supplementary data corresponding to  
different operating frequencies of same designs, enhancing the model’s  
overall performance and versatility.  
[11] R. Liang, Z. Xie, J. Jung, V. Chauha, Y. Chen, J. Hu, H. Xiang, G.-J. Nam,  
Routing-free crosstalk prediction, in: 2020 IEEE/ACM International Conference  
on Computer Aided Design, ICCAD, IEEE, 2020, pp. 1–9.  
[12] M. Chentouf, C. Naimy, Z.E.A.A. Ismaili, Machine learning application for early  
power analysis accuracy improvement: A case study for cells switching power, in:  
2021 International Conference on Microelectronics, ICM, IEEE, 2021, pp. 17–20.  
[13] Z. Guo, M. Liu, J. Gu, S. Zhang, D.Z. Pan, Y. Lin, A timing engine inspired graph  
neural network model for pre-routing slack prediction, 2022.  
[14] A.B. Kahng, M. Luo, S. Nath, SI for free: machine learning of interconnect
